coredata.dc:title: High performance of carbon quantum dots-decorated-polymeric nanocomposite for enhanced microwave adsorption of vanadium (V) from water
coredata.dc:creator: Mahmoud, Mohamed E.
Abdelfattah, Amir M.
Fekry, Nesma A.
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDots), as a sorbent has been decorated via grafting onto polymer matrix (PHQFB) based on 8-hydroxyquinoline (HQ) and biuret using formaldehyde cross-linker for the construction of a novel nanosorbent (CQDots@PHQFB). The assembled material was characterized by different techniques. The SEM image of the prepared CQDots@PHQFB nanosorbent indicated its particle size in the range 17.6–24.1 nm and the average diameter of CQDots@PHQFB is 14.9 nm. The resultant BET pore volume (0.028 cm3 g−1), the mean pore diameter (11.3nm) and surface area (11.5 m2 g−1) were computed. Adsorption studies were accomplished to evaluate the V(V) removal by CQDots@PHQFB nanosorbent from aqueous solution by microwave irradiation technique. CQDots@PHQFB demonstrated excellent adsorption and selectivity for V(V) ions from acidic medium (91.2% for 5 mg L−1) at low pH conditions (pH 1–3). Therefore, three different mechanisms were proposed to account for such high extraction of V(V). The adsorption kinetics of V(V) was approved by the pseudo-second order with of R2 (1.0) for both concentrations (5 and 15 mg L−1). Furthermore, The values of qe(cal.) (18.3 mg g−1, 54.3 mg g−1 for 5, 15 mg L−1, respectively), were also confirmed to be very close to qe(exp.) (18.2 mg g−1, 54.2 mg g−1 for 5, 15 mg L−1, respectively), while the equilibrium isotherm results were evaluated by nonlinear models, viz. Langmuir, Freundlich, Liu and Temkin. The maximum capacity of adsorption from the Liu model was found 219mg g−1 at 346 K The computed parameters of thermodynamic approved endothermic and spontaneous nature of V(V) adsorption onto CQDots@PHQFB nanosorbent. V(V) ions removal values corresponding to 99.3% and 97.7% were achieved from sea water and wastewater, respectively. The collected results affirm that the designed CQDots@PHQFB nanosorbent is an excellent candidate adsorptive uptake of V(V) from aqueous medium. The extraordinary adsorption abilities with higher reusability features for adsorption of inorganic pollutant such as V(V) entrusts this CQDots@PHQFB as a potential economical adsorbent to alleviate water pollution problem.

head.ce:title: High performance of carbon quantum dots-decorated-polymeric nanocomposite for enhanced microwave adsorption of vanadium (V) from water

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Sciences, Chemistry Department, Alexandria University, Moharem Bey, Alexandria, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Sciences, Chemistry Department, Alexandria University, Moharem Bey, Alexandria, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDots), as a sorbent has been decorated via grafting onto polymer matrix (PHQFB) based on 8-hydroxyquinoline (HQ) and biuret using formaldehyde cross-linker for the construction of a novel nanosorbent (CQDots@PHQFB). The assembled material was characterized by different techniques. The SEM image of the prepared CQDots@PHQFB nanosorbent indicated its particle size in the range 17.6–24.1 nm and the average diameter of CQDots@PHQFB is 14.9 nm. The resultant BET pore volume (0.028 cm3 g−1), the mean pore diameter (11.3nm) and surface area (11.5 m2 g−1) were computed. Adsorption studies were accomplished to evaluate the V(V) removal by CQDots@PHQFB nanosorbent from aqueous solution by microwave irradiation technique. CQDots@PHQFB demonstrated excellent adsorption and selectivity for V(V) ions from acidic medium (91.2% for 5 mg L−1) at low pH conditions (pH 1–3). Therefore, three different mechanisms were proposed to account for such high extraction of V(V). The adsorption kinetics of V(V) was approved by the pseudo-second order with of R2 (1.0) for both concentrations (5 and 15 mg L−1). Furthermore, The values of qe(cal.) (18.3 mg g−1, 54.3 mg g−1 for 5, 15 mg L−1, respectively), were also confirmed to be very close to qe(exp.) (18.2 mg g−1, 54.2 mg g−1 for 5, 15 mg L−1, respectively), while the equilibrium isotherm results were evaluated by nonlinear models, viz. Langmuir, Freundlich, Liu and Temkin. The maximum capacity of adsorption from the Liu model was found 219mg g−1 at 346 K The computed parameters of thermodynamic approved endothermic and spontaneous nature of V(V) adsorption onto CQDots@PHQFB nanosorbent. V(V) ions removal values corresponding to 99.3% and 97.7% were achieved from sea water and wastewater, respectively. The collected results affirm that the designed CQDots@PHQFB nanosorbent is an excellent candidate adsorptive uptake of V(V) from aqueous medium. The extraordinary adsorption abilities with higher reusability features for adsorption of inorganic pollutant such as V(V) entrusts this CQDots@PHQFB as a potential economical adsorbent to alleviate water pollution problem.

ce:section.ce:para: Vanadium (V) is a vital metal with broad industrial utilization due to its predominant physicochemical features (Tuzen et al., 2013; Hinwood et al., 2015; Zhang et al., 2018a). The increasing worldwide demand for vanadium enhances intensive interests in mining, pigments, pharmaceutical industry, metallurgy and alloys (Nriagu, 1998; Carpentier et al., 2003; Bhatnagar et al., 2008; Anirudhan and Radhakrishnan, 2010; Wang et al., 2012; Leiviskä et al., 2017). Additionally, vanadium is required as an essential element in nutrition microorganisms and cell growth, but it becomes poisonous at high concentrations (above 1 mg L−1) (Patel et al., 1990). Vanadium can exist in the tetravalent and pentavalent forms and its toxicity increases with a higher oxidation state and the most stable oxidation state and highly toxic form of vanadium is V(V) (Berman, 1980; Filik and Yanaz, 2009; Sirviö et al., 2016). Therefore, long-exposure to vanadium can cause cancer, paralysis, anemia, bronchopneumonia and damage respiratory system (Zhang et al., 2014; Hao et al., 2018; Shaheena et al., 2019). Moreover, the United Nations Environment Program (UNEP), vanadium was classified in the precedence list of hazardous elements (Chen and Liu, 2017). The US Environmental Protection Agency has documented a minimum level of vanadium (0.2 μg L−1), while the accepted concentration of V in potable water source was set in China at 50 μg L−1 (Zhang et al., 2018b).

The toxicity of heavy metals and organic wastes has been dramatically increased in the last decades with a serious impact on water supplies. And that due to human deleterious behaviors including industrial activities, mining, electroplating, steel production, and many others which result in contamination of water bodies subsequently affect biodiversity and consumers leading to acute health problems in addition to possible cancerogenic effects of crops if it used for irrigation purpose (Basheer, 2018). Based on the activities of vanadium in the industrial and mining fields, one must expect that great amounts of residual vanadium would occur in the production water streams and thus transfer to the surrounding environment (Bhatnagar et al., 2008). Therefore, researchers must think to develop processes for the removal and treatment of vanadium in wastewater. However, vanadium is commonly treated by different techniques including ion exchange, cement immobilization, biological, solvent extraction, precipitation, photocatalytic reduction and adsorption methods (Navarro et al., 2007; Naeem et al., 2007; Sturini et al., 2013; Li et al., 2013; Hu et al., 2014; Kaczala et al., 2009; Pennesi et al., 2013). However, the cost-effectiveness of such techniques is questionable. So, the adsorption technique was characterized by positive evidence for water treatment due to feasibility, high efficiency and easy operation (Ali et al., 2018; Al-Shaalan et al., 2019; Narayani et al., 2018; Wei et al., 2018; Kokkinos et al., 2018; Mahmoud and Fekry, 2018; Mahmoud et al., 2020a). A great number of nanomaterials were applied as sorbents for the removal of undesired metal from polluted water (Mahmoud and Fekry, 2018). In this consideration, carbonaceous materials such as CQDots have been used in adsorption studies (Mahmoud et al., 2020a). CQDots represent a new class of nanomaterial which is characterized by a large number of surface oxygenated groups (Lim et al., 2015). CQDots possess an extraordinary number of reactive (-OH) groups compared to carbon which can be renewed to more explicit adsorption sites (Mahmoud et al., 2020a). In addition, the CQDots has small size, high surface area, low toxicity, easy functionalization and low costs to provide excellent candidates in various adsorption processes (Mahmoud et al., 2020b). Recently, CQDots have been reported hot research topics due to their new promotes functionalities that consequently hold potential in a variety of applications in electrochemistry, photocatalysis, bioanalysis (Ding et al., 2014; Song et al., 2014; Feng and Qian, 2018; Hill and Galan et al., 2017). Moreover, CQDots have been recently applied in the detection, sensitivity, selectivity and adsorption of metal ions from water (Mahmoud et al., 2020a, 2020b).

To overcome the technical challenge of utilizing CQDots dispersed-water in adsorption application, advanced research attempts have been dedicated to developing novel CQDots-based composite materials for the removal of hazardous materials (Wang et al., 2017). In the present study, CQDots have been fabricated and decorated via grafting onto synthesized polymer matrix from the reaction of 8-hydroxyquinoline, biuret and formaldehyde cross-linker (PHQFB) for the creation of a novel (CQDots@PHQFB). The assembled with its incorporated various functionalities are aimed to employ for selective adsorption of V(V) ions from aqueous solution. CQDots@PHQFB has been characterized by different techniques and an explanation of its adsorption capability toward V(V) ions has been established by the microwave technique. Various kinetic models, thermodynamics and isotherm models have been also applied to interpret the collected experimental data for adsorption of V(V) onto CQDots@PHQFB. Finally, the investigated nanosorbent has been mainly devoted to potential adsorptive V(V) removal from sea water and wastewater.
All chemicals and instruments are itemized as Table 1 .

A microwave pyrolysis procedure for starch decomposition was used for the formation of CQDots (Das et al., 2018). One gram of starch was mixed with 20 mL water and this mix was irradiated in a microwave oven (Mw). After 15 s, the solution turned into chocolate color due to the production of solid CQDots. The CQDots was rinsed with distilled water a number of times and centrifuged to expel residual materials. Next, the CQDots was dried at 60 °C. The CQDots was confirmed by fluorescence spectrophotometer. The supreme emission of CQDots can be stabilized at 526 nm during the wave length of excitation 390 nm (Fig. S1). The highest absorbance of CQDots was 280 nm.

To fabricate CQDots@PHQFB nanosorbent, a mixture of 0.1 mol of HQ, 0.1 mol Biuret and 0.2 mol formaldehyde was refluxed till converted into reddish liquid. 7.0 g of CQDots was suspended into H2O and then added in a round flask and after 8 h, the CQDots@PHQFB nanosorbent was produced. Afterward, CQDots@PHQFB was rinsed with hot H2O and centrifuged. Finally, the CQDots@PHQFB nanosorbent was dried at 60 °C for 24 h. Scheme S1 shows stages of synthesis of CQDots@PHQFB nanosorbent.
All experiments were explained in details in the supplemental section.

The FT-IR analysis was utilized to distinguish the functional groups in CQDots as represented in Fig. S2(a). A broad peak at 3320 cm−1 was attributed to –OH and another peak at 2919 cm−1 suggested the existence –CH2 group. The spectrum of CQDots displayed other distinguishing peaks at 1704 cm−1(-CO in carboxylic acid), 1638 cm−1(-CC-), 1411 cm−1(-CH2) and 1012 cm−1(-C-O-) as previously described (Mahmoud et al., 2020a). Fig. S2(b) shows a specific broad band at 3352 cm−1 for (-OH) phenolic groups to refer to intermolecular H-bond (Jadhao et al., 2008). Two bands at 3058 and 828 cm−1 may be assigned to the N–H group as imide, while the peaks displayed from 1630 to 1447 cm−1 may be ascribed to the vibration of the aromatic ring. The peak of (-CN) group appeared at 1630 cm−1, while the (–CH2–) bridge group in the polymeric chain may be appeared at 1447, 1372 and 786 cm−1. Finally, four peaks at 955, 1070, 1155 and 1269 cm−1 are ascribed to the 1,2,3,5- substituted benzene ring (Michael et al., 2007). In Fig. S2(c), the two bands at 788 and 1272 cm−1 are attributed to (nitrogen-hydrogen) wag and (carbon-nitrogen) stretch, respectively. However, the intensity and position of 3378, 1632 and 1582 cm−1 peaks were increased and slightly changed which reveals the successful formation of CQDots@PHQFB nanosorbent through interactions of terminal amine group (PHQFB) and hydroxyl group (CQDots). This modification was vital to upsurge the number of surface functional groups that can uptake the vanadium ions from solution.

The morphology of the CQDots@PHQFB is obviously shown on the SEM image (Fig. S3). The prepared CQDots@PHQFB nanosorbent demonstrated smooth exterior surfaces, and regular spherical shapes are well dispersed providing particle size in the range 17.6–24.1 nm. The average diameter of CQDots@PHQFB is 14.9 nm. The dispersion of the particle, as well as the observed porosity, may lead to V(V) adsorption process enhancement. The EDX spectrum of CQDots is shown in Fig.S4(a) and can be utilized to determine the major chemical composition. The EDX of CQDots displayed two peaks which are apportioned to the C and O elements, with atomic percentages of 50.6% and 49.3%, respectively. The EDX spectrum of CQDots@PHQFB is shown in Fig. S4(b). The EDX of CQDots@PHQFB nanosorbent exhibited three peaks which are assigned to the C, O and N elements, with atomic percentages of 63.5%, 18.4% and 17.9%, respectively. Fig. S4(c) displays the N2 adsorption/desorption isotherm of CQDots@PHQFB nanosorbent and this can be classified as a type-III curve (Khalfaoui et al., 2003) to refer to the existence of macroporous structure of CQDots material. The resultant BET pore volume (0.028 cm3 g−1) and mean pore diameter (11.3 nm) were confirmed with the surface area (11.5 m2 g−1) for the designed CQDots@PHQFB nanosorbent.

The thermal stability of CQDots@PHQFB nanosorbent was acquired by TGA as recorded between 0 and 600 °C and Fig. S4(d) shows the thermogram of CQDots@PHQFB. The CQDots@PHQFB exhibits three-steps of dissociation. The first step of dissociation was centered at 26.3–247.2 °C, with weight loss (4.6%), which may be due to the decomposition of (-OH) groups and water molecules (Michael et al., 2007). The second step of dissociation was set at 247.1–482.2 °C, with weight loss (18.6%) that may be due to loss of the quinoline ring and carboxylic groups (Singru and Gurnule, 2010). The third step of decomposition was identified at 482.2–599.8 °C, involving weight loss (46.8%) due to possible decomposition of the aromatic ring.

In the adsorption process, the pH factor is known as a vital controlling feature since it has a critical impact on the point of ionization and speciation of V(V), the charge of surface, alteration of functional groups of CQDots@PHQFB nanosorbent (Peacock and Sherman, 2004). Thus, the impact of pH on the removal effectiveness of V(V) was deliberated in pH 1–10, as shown in Fig. 1 (a). The removal efficiency of both concentrations (5 and 15 mg L−1) was substantively improved by increasing the pH from 1 to 2; (80.8–91.2%) and (75.6–90.1%), respectively, and then declined as pH increased from 3 to 10. The vanadium exists in 12 species in different pH conditions including cationic form (VO2 +) at pH < 2 and anionic species (H2V10O28 4−, VO3(OH)2−, V10O26(OH)2 4−, V2O6(OH)3−, V10O27(OH)5−, V10O28 6−, VO2(OH)2−, VO4 3−, V3O9 3−, V2O7 4−, and V4O12 4−) at pH > 2 to 11 (Post and Robins, 1976). According to the total concentration of vanadium (V) ions at low pH conditions (pH 1–3), two predominant species are existing as VO2 + and H2V10O28 4− ions. Therefore, three different mechanisms are proposed to take place and used to account for the high (%) removal of V(V) from aqueous solution. The first mechanism is related to the possible binding of VO2 + to the surface of CQDots@PHQFB nanosorbent via hydrogen ion exchange from the carboxylic group at pH > 2. The second mechanism is referring to an ion-pair interaction between the protonated surface of CQDots@PHQFB nanosorbent and the anionic species H2V10O28 4− at pH < 3 (Mahmoud et al., 2020c). The third mechanism is based on a complex formation between the lone pair of electrons on the nitrogen or oxygen of PHQFB of VO2 + ions at pH > 3 (Scheme 1 ). The adsorptive removal process of vanadium (V) is strongly supported by these three mechanisms as represented in Fig. S5 at pH < pHPzc (8.04). Above pH 8.0, where pH > pHPzc, the removal of V(V) decreased due to the competition between vanadium anion species and the loaded negatively charged carboxylic acid (–COO-) and (-O-) onto the surface of CQDots@PHQFB. The lowest removal of V(V) was 41.1 and 35.6% at pH 10 using 5 and 15 mg L−1, respectively. In general, the CQDots@PHQFB displayed excellent applicability to adsorb V(V) under various acidic pH levels.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of mass dose of CQDots@PHQFB nanosorbent on V(V) adsorption
ce:section.ce:para: The impact of nanosorbent quantity on V(V) adsorption by CQDots@PHQFB is shown in Fig. 1(b). The CQDots@PHQFB dose is an important factor in the identification of adsorption percentage and adsorption capacity. The percentage of removal of V(V) was increased from 62.6 to 99.4% (for 5 mg L−1) and from 57.5 to 98.1% (for 15 mg L−1), as CQDots@PHQFB nanosorbent dose was increased from 1 to 20 mg. In addition, The results designated at two concentrations of V(V) that by increasing the CQDots@PHQFB nanosorbent contents from 1 to 20 mg, the percentage removal of V(V) rises but adsorption capacity declines. The increasing in removal percentage may be interpreted by the increased surface area of CQDots@PHQFB nanosorbent and the accessibility of extra adsorption active sites whereas the declining in adsorption capability is owing to the actuality that some of the adsorption positions may remain unsaturated throughout the adsorption of V(V) onto CQDots@PHQFB nanosorbent (Mahmoud et al., 2020b).

The rate of process and dynamic performance of the adsorption system are very significant issues for strategic procedure control (Martins et al., 2015). Therefore, the adsorption behavior of V(V) onto CQDots@PHQFB nanosorbent may proceed via 3 stages. (i) Passage of the V(V) from bulk solution to the external surface of CQDots@PHQFB nanosorbent (diffusion of film), (ii) conveyance into CQDots@PHQFB nanosorbent by either surface sawing and/or pore sawing and (iii) adsorption of V(V) onto CQDots@PHQFB nanosorbent. The slowest of these steps is generally referred to detect the overall rate of the adsorption reaction of V(V) onto CQDots@PHQFB nanosorbent. Adsorption kinetics displays a robust dependence on the chemical and/or physical features of the CQDots@PHQFB nanosorbent which also impacts on elucidating the sorption mechanism. The kinetic models of CQDots@PHQFB nanosorbent were investigated to suitable with the characterized experimental records and the computed parameters from Table 2 are itemized in Table 3
.

As listed, the pseudo-first order and Elovich were not appropriate to designate the experimental data, awarding lowest R2 (0.878–0.830) for 5 mg L−1 and (0.526–0.969) for 15 mg L−1. The pseudo-second order and intraparticle diffusion models were found quite fitted. In contrast, the pseudo-second order model were provided with the highest values of R2 (1.0) for both concentrations (5 and 15 mg L−1) Furthermore, The values of qe(cal.) (18.3 mg g−1, 54.3 mg g−1 for 5, 15 mg L−1, respectively), were also confirmed to be very close to qe(exp.) (18.2 mg g−1, 54.2 mg g−1 for 5, 15 mg L−1, respectively).Thus, this model was well suited to describe the adsorption reaction of V(V) onto CQDots@PHQFB nanosorbent as explained in Fig. 1 (c). The outlined data and results refer to the possible adsorption of V(V) onto CQDots@PHQFB nanosorbent via chemisorption that may be the rate-controlled step (Omidinasab et al., 2018). The rate-limiting (Kid) values for adsorption of V(V) as computed from the model of intraparticle diffusion were 0.77 and 2.73 mg g−1 min−1/2 by using 5 and 15 mg L−1 of V(V). The line did not pass the origin and therefore indicated that the intraparticle diffusion is not just the rate controlling stage (Mahmoud and Fekry, 2018).

Equilibrium isotherms verify the effect of the adsorbate concentration on the adsorption operation and the relationship between the degree of accretion of V(V) onto the surface of CQDots@PHQFB to the concentration of V(V) at different specific reaction temperatures (Jauris et al., 2017). In this study, the experimental data were investigated and described by Langmuir, Freundlich, Temkin and Liu models.

The Langmuir isotherm assumes that the adsorbates can be chemically adsorbed at finite number of well-defined positions. Each position can only hold one adsorbate species and all positions of adsorbent have the same energy, while the interactions between the adsorbate species are neglected (Langmuir, 1918).

The Freundlich isotherm supposes that the concentration of the adsorbate on the surface of adsorbent increases with increasing the concentration of adsorbate. Theoretically, using this model, an unlimited quantity of adsorption can take place (Freundlich, 1906; Nodeh et al., 2016). The Liu isotherm is amalgamation of Langmuir and Freundlich models; consequently, the monolayer hypothesis of Langmuir is overruled and unlimited adsorption hypothesis of Freundlich is also eliminated (Liu et al., 2003; Prola et al., 2013).

The Liu isotherm expects that the active positions of the adsorbent are not energetically similar. Therefore, the adsorbate ions may prefer some active sites for occupation onto adsorbent (Liu et al., 2003). Though, saturation of the active positions should take place unlike in the Freundlich isotherm. Taking into account that the CQDots@PHQFB has various functional groups as discussed in the characterization section, therefore, the active sites of CQDots@PHQFB are predicted not to have the same energy.

Temkin isotherm was appointed to depict the interactions between adsorbate (V(V)) and the adsorbent (CQDots@PHQFB) and this isotherm expects that the temperature (T) of all particles through process of adsorption decline when the superficies of (CQDots@PHQFB) is extremely wrapped by adsorbate (V(V) (Mahmoud et al., 2020a, 2020d, 2020e).

Fig. 2 (a) displays the adsorption isotherms of V(V) onto the surface of CQDots@PHQFB nanosorbent at 346 K. Considering the values of R2 Adj and SD (Jauris et al., 2017), Liu model was found the most fitting approach for adsorption V(V) onto CQDots@PHQFB nanosorbent with the indication of the closeness of Qmax was closest to resultant data. The SD values of Langmuir, Freundlich and Temkin were ranged from 0.94 to 5.69, 18.0 to 19.2 and 5.69 to 7.83, respectively. The R2 Adj values of Langmuir, Freundlich and Temkin were ranged from 0.992 to 0.999, 0.914 to 0.937 and 0.986 to 0.993, respectively. The Liu model exhibited the highest R2 Adj values corresponding to 0.999 and the lowest SD values reached 0.94 at 346 K. The highest R2 Adj and lowest SD prove that the adsorption process of V(V) onto CQDots@PHQFB nanosorbent was mostly obeying the Liu model (de Oliveira Carvalho et al., 2019).

As outlined in Table 4 , the capacity of adsorption of V(V) generally was amplified by increasing the reaction temperature. It is evident that the temperature promoted the adsorption of V(V) onto CQDots@PHQFB nanosorbent. At 346 K, the Qmax of V(V) onto CQDots@PHQFB by Liu model was characterized as 219 mg g−1. This value confirms that CQDots@PHQFB nanosorbent is a superb adsorbent for V(V) sorption from aqueous solutions.

Thermodynamic study for the adsorption of V(V) onto CQDots@PHQFB nanosorbent was investigated in the temperature range between 303 and 346 K. The equilibrium ke can be elaborated from the most fitted isotherm model based on Eq. (12) (Lima et al., 2019). Fig. 2 (b) shows Liu isotherm models in different temperatures that were used to calculate Ke and draw the relation between Ke and 1000/T (Fig. 1(d)). The thermodynamic values for adsorption of V(V) onto CQDots@PHQFB nanosorbent are provided in Table 5 . The positive value of ΔHo (11.85 kJ mol−1) is less than 20 kJ mol−1, indicating that the adsorption of V(V) onto CQDots@PHQFB was endothermic and physisorption (Machado et al., 2016; Jauris et al., 2017). The positive values of ΔSo indicate that V(V) ions had exhibited a high preference for adsorption on the surface of CQDots@PHQFB nanosorbent. The ΔGo value is important for explanation the thermodynamic procedure of adsorption with respect to the spontaneity of adsorption process (Ali et al., 2019). A higher negative value of ΔGo was monitored for adsorption of V(V) on the surface of CQDots@PHQFB nanosorbent, which designates more favorable adsorption. Thus, the adsorptive process of V(V) onto CQDots@PHQFB nanosorbent was energetically favorable at all temperatures. 3.2.6 Regeneration study of CQDots@PHQFB nanosorbent.

From the economical point of view, reusability of any adsorbent is a necessary step for increasing of the successive usage of an adsorbent. Herein, 0.1 M of HCl was utilized as recycling reagent for regeneration of CQDots@PHQFB to maintain the original function groups on the surface. As predictable, the efficacy of CQDots@PHQFB for removal V(V) after repeating five sequential cycles of adsorption-desorption was evident as shown in Fig. 3 . Fascinatingly, the percentage removal of V(V) was practically constant for the first, second and third cycles with about only 0.8% change in the V(V) removal proficiency. The % removal of V(V) was declined from 90.4 to 80.1 after four cycle. After the five adsorption cycles the scarcity in adsorption was about 10.3%. The reduction in adsorption efficiency of CQDots@PHQFB after five successive regeneration series can be ascribed to the loss of active positions and occlusion of some pores in CQDots@PHQFB. Thus the results displayed the suitable reusability of CQDots@PHQFB for removal of V(V).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application CQDots@PHQFB nanosorbent for V(V) removal from real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The validity of the application of CQDots@PHQFB nanosorbent for extraction of speciation of V(V) from real specimens is a actual significant stage that necessity to assess. So, CQDots@PHQFB nanosorbent was emphasized that applicability for removal of V(V) from several water specimens at a small time (1 min) by microwave adsorption technique. The recovery of V(V) were 97.7% and 99.3% from wastewater and sea water at pH 2.0, respectively. At original pH of samples, the first extraction results were 50.2% and 60.8% for wastewater (pH 7.71) and sea water (pH 8.65), respectively. The second extraction results were 63.5% and 66.8% for wastewater and sea water, respectively. The third extraction results were 74.3% and 80.1% for wastewater and sea water, respectively. Noticeable, the pH of samples is a vital factor for removing of V(V) by adsorption process. Table S1, the compared results confirm that designed CQDots@PHQFB nanosorbent have the potential, selectivity and validation for removal of V(V) from different water samples under strongly acidic conditions.

The modified carbon quantum dots (CQDots) via grafting onto polymer matrix (PHQFB) for assembly of CQDots@PHQFB was confirmed as an excellent nanosorbent for recovery and removal of V(V). The produced CQDots@PHQFB nanosorbent (14.9 nm diameter) shows very fast adsorption process in seconds via microwave sorption techniques. The maximum capacity value was 219 mg g−1 at 346 K according to Liu model. The maximum removals of V(V) ions were confirmed at pH 2.0 as 91.2% and 90.4% for 5 and 15 mg L−1, respectively. Regrading dosage effect it reached steady state consuming 10 mg of the nanosorbent with about 91% removal for both concentrations, Thermodynamic parameters approved the spontaneous and endothermic nature with ΔHo = 11.85 kJ mol−1 and ΔSo = 98.6. About regeneration and reusability, only 0.8% change on the removal efficiency after first, second and third cycle. Application of the nanosorbent for V(V) removal from sea water and wastewater were highly achieved 99.3% and 97.7%, respectively. Finally, the collected results reveal that the designed CQDots@PHQFB nanosorbent is an excellent candidate for adsorptive uptake of V(V) from the aqueous condition.
